text: retail sales volumes grew by 0.3% in july, more slowly than the 0.7% monthly average in the first half of the year, but this was nevertheless sufficient in combination with the strong growth in may and june to keep the three-month (on previous three months) growth rate on a rising trend.

claude_trace.reads: Sees a mixed surface: '0.3% in July' described as 'more slowly than the 0.7% monthly average' (soft cue) alongside 'strong growth in May and June' and 'rising trend' (firm cue). The adverb 'nevertheless' and 'sufficient' read as hedging.
claude_trace.concepts_identified: UK retail sales, month-on-month deceleration, three-month moving average, consumer spending momentum.
claude_trace.weighing: Claude weighs the headline slowdown (0.3% vs 0.7%) against the three-month trend and concludes the passage is simply describing data without a stance verdict. With both a slowdown fact and a strength fact present, and no explicit policy verb, Claude defaults to 'neutral' as the safe read.
claude_trace.why_wrong: Claude treats the sentence as data description rather than as a BoE MPC stance signal. In the MPC convention, 'three-month growth rate on a rising trend' for retail sales is a demand-side firming signal that maps to hawkish: consumer demand is accelerating on the preferred smoothed measure, which pressures capacity and inflation. Claude's aversion to committing — paired with the 'more slowly' clause — pulls it to neutral and it misses the directional verdict the MPC is actually encoding.

abel_trace.step1_classify: macro_direct — UK consumer demand print; macro nodes activated: {GDP, inflation, unemployment, federalFunds (BoE Bank Rate proxy)}. Primary node: consumption component of GDP.
abel_trace.step2_hypotheses: H1 (hawkish): 3-month rising trend in retail sales indicates demand momentum above trend → inflationary pressure → tilt toward tightening. H2 (dovish): July 0.3% is a step-down from 0.7% average → consumer is fatiguing → tilt toward easing. H3 (neutral): mixed print, no directional verdict. H4 (contrarian): rising 3m/3m with a soft monthly suggests base-effect noise that MPC would discount.
abel_trace.step3_graph_discovery: Markov blanket: retail_sales → consumption → GDP → output_gap → inflation → BankRate. Activated nodes: consumption momentum, output gap. BoE-specific prior: MPC explicitly prefers the 3m-on-3m smoothed measure over single-month prints in minutes language; the phrase 'rising trend' is a stylized MPC verdict, not neutral description. Data-print convention: 'rising trend' on the preferred smoother = demand firming.
abel_trace.step4_verify: Direction check: rising 3m/3m retail sales → positive consumption impulse → narrowing output gap → upward inflation pressure → hawkish tilt on BankRate. Chain is sign-consistent. H2 fails because MPC down-weights single-month July vs the smoothed series it just highlighted. H3 fails because the clause structure ('nevertheless sufficient... to keep... on a rising trend') is a verdict, not a wash.
abel_trace.step5_web_grounding: BoE MPC minutes historically use the phrase 'rising trend' in the preferred 3m/3m form as a demand-side firming signal; August 2005-style minute blocks with this exact construction consistently preceded hawkish-leaning commentary on consumer demand. The MPC's Inflation Report framework ties sustained consumer demand above trend to upside CPI risk relative to the 2% target.
abel_trace.step6_synthesize: H1 wins: the sentence survives the monthly-softness clause and delivers a verdict that the preferred smoothed measure is firming. Under the Markov blanket this propagates to inflation-upside → hawkish. H3 is rejected because the sentence is not balanced — it is concessive ('nevertheless sufficient'), which in MPC prose is a tell that the firmer reading prevails.
abel_trace.why_correct: Abel's graph forces explicit directional propagation: a rising 3m/3m consumption proxy cannot be neutral once it enters the output-gap→inflation chain. The BoE-specific prior about the MPC's preferred smoothed measure turns the concessive clause ('more slowly... but nevertheless') into a hawkish tell rather than a wash.

comparison: Claude pattern-matches surface hedging ('more slowly', 'nevertheless') and defaults to neutral whenever the text contains both a soft and a firm data point. Abel's Markov blanket refuses that escape hatch: once retail_sales is mapped to consumption→output_gap→inflation, the chain must resolve to a sign. The BoE-specific data-print convention — that MPC minutes use 3m/3m 'rising trend' as the actual stance-bearing phrase — is exactly the kind of institutional prior that Abel's web-grounding step supplies and Claude's generic reading lacks, which is why the structural graph pipeline flips this case.

text: conditions elsewhere in the euro area were generally rather more robust, although business confidence was less buoyant even in france and euro-area industrial production had fallen in january; a slowdown in the telecommunications sector might also hold back growth.

claude_trace.reads: Sees a balance: 'rather more robust' (positive) against 'less buoyant', 'industrial production had fallen', 'slowdown in the telecommunications sector', 'hold back growth' (negative). Notices qualifiers 'generally', 'might'.
claude_trace.concepts_identified: Euro-area growth, business confidence, industrial production, sector-specific (telecoms) drag, spillovers to UK.
claude_trace.weighing: Claude sees a 'robust... although... fell... might hold back' structure and reads it as descriptive: the positive clause and the multiple negative clauses appear to cancel. Without an explicit BoE policy verb, it assigns 'neutral'.
claude_trace.why_wrong: Claude misreads the rhetorical architecture. The 'generally rather more robust' clause is a concession that is then dismantled by three concrete negatives: falling IP, weaker confidence (even in France), and a forward-looking sector drag. In MPC prose, the weight is always on the concrete evidence block, and 'might also hold back growth' is explicitly a downside-risk flag to UK growth via euro-area spillovers — a dovish cue for Bank Rate. Claude's symmetric reading ignores that BoE minutes weight forward-looking downside risks heavily.

abel_trace.step1_classify: proxy_routed — external demand proxy for UK. Primary macro nodes: {GDP (UK, via euro-area demand spillover), inflation (import/tradables channel), federalFunds (BoE Bank Rate)}.
abel_trace.step2_hypotheses: H1 (dovish): euro-area IP falling + confidence softer + telecom drag → weaker external demand for UK exports → downside UK GDP → dovish tilt. H2 (hawkish): 'rather more robust' headline → external demand firm → supports UK growth. H3 (neutral): mixed signals cancel. H4 (contrarian): telecom is idiosyncratic sector noise that MPC should discount; concession clause dominates.
abel_trace.step3_graph_discovery: Markov blanket: euro_area_IP → UK_external_demand → UK_GDP → UK_output_gap → UK_inflation → BankRate. Activated nodes: external demand channel, import-price channel. BoE-specific prior: MPC minutes from the early-2000s telecom bust era consistently treated euro-area IP contractions as UK downside risk, not noise. The conjunction 'although' in MPC prose flips the polarity of the preceding clause.
abel_trace.step4_verify: Direction check: falling euro-area IP + softer French confidence + telecom drag → negative external demand impulse → widening UK output gap → disinflationary pressure → dovish BankRate tilt. Chain sign-consistent. H2 fails because the 'although' concession inversion is explicit. H3 fails because three downside specifics outweigh one generic positive. H4 fails because MPC treats telecom sector drag as a systemic growth risk in this era, not as sector noise.
abel_trace.step5_web_grounding: BoE MPC minutes around the 2001-2002 telecom/dot-com downturn explicitly cited euro-area industrial production falls and France-specific confidence weakness as downside risks informing dovish Bank Rate decisions. MPC convention: when UK's largest trading partner shows IP contraction, it feeds through to UK GDP forecasts within two quarters.
abel_trace.step6_synthesize: H1 wins. Under the Markov blanket, a negative external demand shock propagates to lower UK inflation and a dovish BankRate tilt. The rhetorical architecture ('although... fallen... might also hold back') is MPC shorthand for downside risk acknowledgement, which in stance-space is dovish.
abel_trace.why_correct: Abel's graph forces Claude's 'wash' into a directional read because falling euro-area IP cannot enter UK_GDP with zero sign. The BoE-specific prior about 'although' as a polarity-flip and about telecom drag as a systemic rather than idiosyncratic signal are exactly the institutional cues that the web-grounding step supplies.

comparison: Claude treats the sentence as a balanced mixed signal and defaults to neutral. Abel's pipeline cannot accept that: external demand must route through UK_GDP and UK_inflation with an explicit sign, and the dominant signal (IP falling, confidence softening, forward sector drag) is negative. The BoE convention that MPC prose uses 'although' as a polarity flip and weights concrete downside risks over generic headlines is recovered via Abel's web-grounding step — Claude has no mechanism to import that convention, which is why it flips.

text: it was common for these measures to move together, but recent movements in medium-term measures were nevertheless noteworthy, given that oil and gas futures curves were consistent with energy price inflation falling back significantly beyond the near-term horizon.

claude_trace.reads: Sees 'medium-term measures', 'noteworthy', 'oil and gas futures curves', 'energy price inflation falling back significantly beyond the near-term horizon'. The sentence is meta-analytical — it describes measures of inflation expectations rather than making a policy call.
claude_trace.concepts_identified: Inflation expectations measures (likely break-evens or surveys), oil/gas futures curves, energy price inflation mean-reversion, near-term vs medium-term horizon.
claude_trace.weighing: Claude sees technical/descriptive language about expectations measures and futures curves. Without an explicit 'MPC will...' verb or a clear 'too high/too low' judgment, Claude reads it as observational and assigns neutral.
claude_trace.why_wrong: Claude misses that the content is unambiguously disinflationary: energy futures curves implying inflation 'falling back significantly' beyond the near-term is a direct downside input to the BoE's medium-term inflation forecast, which is the MPC's target variable. The adjective 'noteworthy' for medium-term measures is MPC-speak for 'this matters for our Bank Rate path.' Claude's surface-level read of 'technical measures = neutral' misses that every token in the sentence points to lower inflation.

abel_trace.step1_classify: macro_direct — inflation expectations + energy commodity input. Primary macro nodes: {inflation (medium-term), federalFunds (BoE Bank Rate), GDP (via real income channel secondary)}.
abel_trace.step2_hypotheses: H1 (dovish): futures curves pricing energy disinflation + medium-term expectation measures moving down → lower medium-term CPI path → dovish BankRate tilt. H2 (hawkish): 'noteworthy' movement in medium-term expectations could mean expectations drifting up (de-anchoring) → hawkish. H3 (neutral): purely descriptive meta-analysis. H4 (contrarian): near-term energy remains elevated even if futures curve slopes down, so near-term CPI still overshoots.
abel_trace.step3_graph_discovery: Markov blanket: energy_futures → headline_CPI → medium_term_inflation_expectations → BankRate. Activated nodes: energy pass-through, inflation-expectation anchoring, MPC 2% target. BoE-specific prior: MPC cares about the 2-year-ahead inflation projection almost exclusively; 'medium-term measures' is the policy-relevant horizon. Data-print convention: 'falling back significantly beyond the near-term horizon' directly maps to the MPC's forecast-target object.
abel_trace.step4_verify: Direction check: oil/gas futures sloping down → lower future headline CPI → medium-term inflation expectations drift lower → inflation path falls below or toward target → dovish BankRate tilt. Sign consistent. H2 fails because the sentence specifies futures curves 'consistent with energy price inflation falling back' — the direction of medium-term movement is explicitly downward. H4 fails because the MPC targets the medium term, not near-term, so near-term stickiness doesn't overturn the call.
abel_trace.step5_web_grounding: BoE MPC framework: the 2% CPI target is assessed at the 2-year horizon. When oil/gas futures curves imply energy disinflation beyond the near-term, the MPC has historically used this to justify looking through near-term CPI overshoots and holding or cutting Bank Rate. This construction appears in minutes from 2008, 2011, and 2022 energy-driven episodes.
abel_trace.step6_synthesize: H1 wins. The whole sentence is a construction of a dovish medium-term inflation argument: futures imply energy disinflation → medium-term expectations drop → medium-term CPI forecast falls → Bank Rate tilts dovish. The Markov blanket gives zero support to H3 once 'falling back significantly' is read as an input to the inflation node.
abel_trace.why_correct: Abel's pipeline reads 'medium-term' as the MPC's policy-relevant horizon rather than as generic jargon, and treats 'falling back significantly' as a direct sign-bearing input to the inflation node. The graph refuses to let a downward-sloping energy futures curve enter the system as 'neutral'.

comparison: Claude sees meta-analytical language ('measures', 'noteworthy', 'futures curves') and treats it as descriptive, missing that the content is a direct dovish input to the MPC's target forecast. Abel's pipeline maps the sentence to specific macro nodes with explicit signs, and crucially knows that 'medium-term' is the BoE's policy-relevant horizon — not a hedge word. The BoE data-print convention that 2-year-ahead inflation is the stance-bearing object is the institutional prior that Abel's web-grounding step surfaces; Claude's generic read has no way to privilege 'medium-term' over 'near-term' and so collapses to neutral.

text: one possible counterpart to the recent revisions to the current account might be weaker estimates of the financial position of the corporate sector, but corporate balance sheets had recently been improving and appeared well-positioned to accommodate higher levels of investment.

claude_trace.reads: Sees 'possible counterpart', 'might be weaker estimates', 'but... improving', 'well-positioned to accommodate higher levels of investment'. Identifies hedged language ('possible', 'might') and a balanced structure.
claude_trace.concepts_identified: Current account revisions, corporate sector financial position, corporate balance sheets, investment capacity.
claude_trace.weighing: Claude reads this as an analytical aside about accounting identities: current account revisions → implication for corporate sector → but balance sheets are fine. It's discussing the coherence of national accounts data, not making a stance claim. Claude assigns neutral.
claude_trace.why_correct: This is a genuinely observational passage about national-accounts bookkeeping consistency. The MPC is working through whether a current-account revision implies a corporate-balance-sheet weakness, and concluding 'no, the sector is fine.' There's no policy verdict — just reconciliation of statistical revisions. 'Neutral' is the right call because the sentence doesn't move either the inflation path or the growth path in a stance-bearing direction.

abel_trace.step1_classify: macro_direct — corporate sector financial position; nodes activated: {GDP (via investment), federalFunds (BoE Bank Rate)}.
abel_trace.step2_hypotheses: H1 (hawkish): 'well-positioned to accommodate higher levels of investment' → stronger capex pipeline → demand impulse → hawkish tilt. H2 (dovish): current account revisions could imply weaker corporate position → downside investment. H3 (neutral): observational reconciliation of national accounts, no stance. H4 (contrarian): 'higher levels of investment' is conditional ('appeared well-positioned to') not forecast.
abel_trace.step3_graph_discovery: Markov blanket: corporate_balance_sheets → investment → GDP → output_gap → inflation → BankRate. Activated nodes: investment, output gap. BoE-specific prior: improving corporate balance sheets + capacity for higher investment historically feeds through to capex growth within 4-6 quarters.
abel_trace.step4_verify: Direction check: balance sheets improving → investment capacity rising → demand impulse → narrowing output gap → upward inflation pressure → hawkish BankRate tilt. Chain is sign-consistent — so H1 looks to win.
abel_trace.step5_web_grounding: Periods when UK corporate balance sheets improved (e.g., mid-2000s, mid-2010s) often preceded capex cycles that supported hawkish-leaning MPC discussions. This historical pattern reinforces the H1 reading.
abel_trace.step6_synthesize: Abel chains corporate_balance_sheets → investment → inflation and concludes hawkish. It over-weights the conditional 'well-positioned to accommodate higher levels of investment' as a forward capex signal rather than reading it as a reassurance about statistical-revision coherence.
abel_trace.why_wrong: Abel's Markov blanket forces a directional read on any sentence that touches an activated macro node, even when the sentence is a reconciliation of statistical revisions rather than a forecast. 'Well-positioned to accommodate' is hypothetical capacity, not a forecast of realized investment. Abel mistakes the clarifying 'but corporate balance sheets had recently been improving' — which is there to rule out a dovish tail risk from the current-account revision — as an active hawkish signal. The pipeline lacks a 'this is observational, not stance-bearing' escape hatch.

comparison: This is the failure mode of forcing graph-directional reads on genuinely observational text. Claude's willingness to sit at neutral when the sentence is a national-accounts reconciliation is exactly right; Abel's Markov blanket treats any positive adjective attached to an activated node as a sign-bearing input and over-commits. BoE MPC minutes contain a lot of this statistical-coherence prose that has no stance content, and Abel's discipline to always produce a signed propagation turns into a liability here. Claude wins on genuinely neutral text precisely because it lacks the machinery that otherwise helps Abel.

text: it remained the case, however, that changes in term premia had accounted for only a small proportion of the overall rise in longer-term advanced economy government bond yields over the past two years, relative to the contribution from the rise in expected policy rates.

claude_trace.reads: Sees 'term premia', 'small proportion of the overall rise', 'longer-term advanced economy government bond yields', 'expected policy rates'. This is a yield-decomposition exercise: how much of the yield rise is term premium vs expected rates.
claude_trace.concepts_identified: Yield curve decomposition, term premium vs expectations component, advanced-economy sovereign yields, policy rate expectations.
claude_trace.weighing: Claude recognizes a technical accounting statement: 'of the rise in yields, most was expectations, little was term premium.' This is a statement about market microstructure / yield attribution, not a BoE stance claim. Claude assigns neutral.
claude_trace.why_correct: This passage is a backward-looking attribution of the past two years' yield rise across advanced economies — it's commenting on why yields rose, not on what the MPC intends to do. The MPC is describing the market's interpretation of its own past actions (expected policy rates did the work, not term premia). That's descriptive analysis of market dynamics, not a forward stance signal. Neutral is correct.

abel_trace.step1_classify: macro_direct — policy rate expectations + bond yields; nodes activated: {federalFunds (BoE Bank Rate), inflation (via real yields secondary)}.
abel_trace.step2_hypotheses: H1 (hawkish): expected policy rates drove the yield rise → market prices further tightening → hawkish. H2 (dovish): term premia compressed could imply disinflation priced in. H3 (neutral): backward-looking yield decomposition, no forward stance. H4 (contrarian): the framing 'remained the case' is a persistence claim about an existing diagnostic, not a new signal.
abel_trace.step3_graph_discovery: Markov blanket: bond_yields → expected_policy_rates → BankRate_path → inflation. Activated nodes: policy-rate expectations, long yields. BoE-specific prior: when MPC minutes emphasize that expected policy rates drove yield rises, they are sometimes signaling that market pricing is consistent with their path.
abel_trace.step4_verify: Direction check: expected policy rates rising (as the dominant component of the 2-year yield rise) → market pricing a higher BankRate path → consistent with hawkish regime. Chain looks sign-consistent for H1.
abel_trace.step5_web_grounding: Post-2022 MPC minutes repeatedly discussed the decomposition of advanced-economy yield rises into expectations vs term premia components. Such discussions often coincided with the MPC's tightening cycle, which biases the 'expected policy rates' framing toward hawkish associations.
abel_trace.step6_synthesize: Abel reads 'the rise was driven by expected policy rates' as a hawkish-aligned market-pricing statement and concludes hawkish.
abel_trace.why_wrong: Abel conflates 'the past two years' rise was driven by expected rates' (a backward-looking decomposition) with 'expected rates are rising now' (a forward-looking stance). The sentence is explicitly retrospective — 'over the past two years' — and is a market-pricing diagnostic, not a forward guidance statement. Abel's graph is biased toward finding sign in any sentence touching BankRate, and here it imposes a forward hawkish read on what is just a yield-attribution exercise. The contrarian H4 ('persistence of an existing diagnostic, not a new signal') is actually the right call and the pipeline under-weighted it.

comparison: Another instance of Abel over-calling direction on a genuinely observational passage — specifically, one that is retrospective yield decomposition rather than forward stance. Claude's instinct that technical market-microstructure statements don't carry stance is correct here. Abel's Markov blanket activates BankRate whenever policy-rate expectations are mentioned and propagates a sign, failing to discount the 'over the past two years' temporal framing. BoE MPC minutes contain frequent descriptive market-function prose like this, and it is precisely the terrain where Abel's always-signed propagation hurts and Claude's neutral-default helps. The harm is structural: the graph has no node for 'this is a backward-looking attribution,' so temporal framing that ought to neutralize the signal is lost.
